coredata.dc:title: Serial nanomaterial synthesis from Ilex paraguariensis biomass for efficient Cr6+ adsorption and detection
coredata.dc:creator: Mercado, D. Fabio
Paredes-Laverde, Marcela
Céspedes, Santiago
Torres-Palma, Ricardo A.
coredata.dc:description: This study presents the use of Ilex paraguariensis, an agro-industrial waste, to obtain multiple nanomaterials through serial processes. The materials synthesized included organic-coated magnetite (Fe3O4@YM), Carbon dots (CD), SiO2 materials, and a magnetite-CD composite (Fe3O4@CD). The characterization of these materials showed that they have nanosphere-like geometries with mean diameters ranging from 4.5 nm to 19.7 nm. The versatility of these materials was demonstrated by using them to develop both Cr6+ adsorbents and sensors. CDs were used as Cr6+ sensors, while all Fe or Si-containing materials were used as Cr6+ adsorbents. CD showed a fluorescence-based method for Cr6+ quantification in the range 1.00–243.00 μM with a detection limit of 0.58 μM. Of all the adsorbents, Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD showed the highest number of adsorption sites at 161.29 and 312.50 mg Cr6+ g−1, which was attributed to the higher surface area and concentration of carboxylic and phenolic groups on the latter material. The economic feasibility of the materials was evaluated through the intensive enhancement price for the adsorption process (φ). It was found that the materials with the highest adsorption capacity are not necessarily the ones with the lowest required economic investment. Fe3O4@YM was shown to have higher economic feasibility considering both synthesis and adsorption processes with values of 8.4 and 5.8 mg Cr6+ USD−1. This approach demonstrates the potential of using agro-industrial residues for the green synthesis of multiple nanomaterials with different applications, useful for fully developing a remediation process.

(For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(b) Thermogravimetric curves. Color guide in (a) and (b): Fe3O4 (black), Fe3O4@YM (red), Fe3O4@CD (blue) SiO2 (orange). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of CD suspended in water at pH = 6.0 (λEXC = 370 nm). (b) Spectrum evolution of CD due to the quenching caused by Cr6+ at pH = 6.0. There is a correlation line on the inset between the quenching and the concentration of Cr6+ in the system.

Comparison between the experimental Cr6+ adsorption with the fitting of the Langmuir, Freundlich, and Redlich- Peterson isotherm models using different nanomaterials as adsorbents. (a) Fe3O4, (b) Fe3O4@YM, (c) Fe3O4@CD, (d) SiO2. Conditions: Cr6+ concentration of 5.14–22.75, 6.19–37.51, 5.88–51.20, and 0.32–17.14 mg L−1 for Fe3O4, Fe3O4@YM, Fe3O4@CD, and SiO2. Adsorbent dose 0.1 g L−1, pH 6.2.

(For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Conditions: Cr6+ concentration of 5.14–22.75, 6.19–37.51, 5.88–51.20, and 0.32–17.14 mg L−1 for Fe3O4, Fe3O4@YM, Fe3O4@CD, and SiO2; respectively. Adsorbent dose 0.1 g L−1, pH 6.2.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents the use of Ilex paraguariensis, an agro-industrial waste, to obtain multiple nanomaterials through serial processes. The materials synthesized included organic-coated magnetite (Fe3O4@YM), Carbon dots (CD), SiO2 materials, and a magnetite-CD composite (Fe3O4@CD). The characterization of these materials showed that they have nanosphere-like geometries with mean diameters ranging from 4.5 nm to 19.7 nm. The versatility of these materials was demonstrated by using them to develop both Cr6+ adsorbents and sensors. CDs were used as Cr6+ sensors, while all Fe or Si-containing materials were used as Cr6+ adsorbents. CD showed a fluorescence-based method for Cr6+ quantification in the range 1.00–243.00 μM with a detection limit of 0.58 μM. Of all the adsorbents, Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD showed the highest number of adsorption sites at 161.29 and 312.50 mg Cr6+ g−1, which was attributed to the higher surface area and concentration of carboxylic and phenolic groups on the latter material. The economic feasibility of the materials was evaluated through the intensive enhancement price for the adsorption process (φ). It was found that the materials with the highest adsorption capacity are not necessarily the ones with the lowest required economic investment. Fe3O4@YM was shown to have higher economic feasibility considering both synthesis and adsorption processes with values of 8.4 and 5.8 mg Cr6+ USD−1. This approach demonstrates the potential of using agro-industrial residues for the green synthesis of multiple nanomaterials with different applications, useful for fully developing a remediation process.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals such as Ni, Hg, Cd, Cr, Mn, Zn, and Pb are increasing in the environment due to the increase in technological and industrial activities (Ahamad et al., 2020b; Naushad et al., 2015). They are considered a threat to the life cycle of animals, plants, and humans due to their low biodegradability and bioaccumulation (Akar et al., 2019; Naushad et al., 2019). Pb causes intellectual abnormalities in children and it might produce hypertension, renal tubular cell destruction, nephropathy, and renal inefficiency (Collin et al., 2022), Hg and Cd cause Minamata and itai-itai diseases (Mitra et al., 2022). Chromium (Cr) is an interesting case, although its trivalent specie is a trace element required for human glucose metabolism, Cr6+ is considered carcinogenic to humans, according to the International Agency for Research on Cancer (Davodpour et al., 2019; Peng and Guo, 2020). It is also teratogenic, and mutagenic and can cause adverse effects such as headache, diarrhea, nausea, and vomiting (Akar et al., 2019). The Cr3+/Cr6+ redox cycle occurs under natural conditions and even under the chlorination process of drinking waters (Lim et al., 1983; Lindsay et al., 2012). The removal of Cr from water bodies has gained the research community attention.

Several methods have been proposed to remove Cr6+ from water bodies: ion exchange, reverse osmosis, microfiltration, photocatalyst, electrocoagulation, and microbial treatments, which are characterized by their simplicity of operation, but some have disadvantages such as rapid saturation, optimum pH operation different from the circumneutral conditions, and the use of dissolved chemical reagents (Peng and Guo, 2020). The adsorption process is interesting since due to its nature, it is an environmentally friendly process with relatively low operating costs. (Karimi-Maleh et al., 2021).

Several materials have been successfully designed as Cr6+ adsorbents. Activated carbons have been extensively studied, but the carbonization and activation steps heavily impact both the production cost and the adsorption performance (Zhao et al., 2020). Due to the development of nanotechnology, more robust materials have shown promising adsorptive properties, such as N or S-doped magnetic carbon aerogels (Ahamad et al., 2019), single-walled and multi-walled carbon nanotubes functionalized with ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid or ionic liquids (Matandabuzo and Ajibade, 2019). But the use of such materials seems unlikely to be applied on a large scale due to the economic feasibility of the process. Non-carbonaceous materials such as Zeolites have also been tested for the same purpose, but their application for adsorption processes is limited by their cage-like structure and the corresponding ion-exchange mechanism (Barea et al., 2014).

Transition metal-based adsorbents have shown interesting properties, especially iron-based nanomaterials, which are non-toxic and have been widely reported for their application in the adsorption process for contaminated water remediation (Cao et al., 2018; Mercado et al., 2018a). Magnetite (Fe3O4) is of interest due to its magnetic properties, which facilitate its removal from the water after the adsorption process. The surface functionalization of Fe3O4 is suggested to increase the adsorption capability (Ahamad et al., 2020a). Artemisia annua L “hairy” roots and Humic acid-coated magnetite showed an enhanced heavy metal adsorption capability compared to the bare Fe3O4 material (Kobylinska et al., 2021; Xue et al., 2021), but due to environmental awareness, the synthesis routes for obtaining Fe3O4 have been modified to include greener practices (Mpongwana and Rathilal, 2022; Sobhanardakani et al., 2018).

Agroindustrial wastes are promising platforms for obtaining materials. Yerba mate (YM) (Ilex paraguariensis) is a native plant of the subtropical region of South America (Gordon-Falconí et al., 2020). Around 350,000 tons per year of YM leaves and branches are processed and commercialized in Paraguay, Argentina, Uruguay, and Brazil to produce a famous type of drink infusions (Gullon et al., 2018), putting the YM in contact with hot water and repeating this procedure several times. The remaining solid is just discarded which results in an environmental issue (Júnior et al., 2020).

YM-wastes have been proven to be useful as a precipitating agent for obtaining iron-oxide nanomaterials (Monje et al., 2022), as a functionalization (Mercado et al., 2018a, 2018b), and even as the feedstock of carbon dots (CD) (Monje et al., 2021), which are spherical-like particles with a mean diameter below 10 nm with intrinsic photoluminescence properties. Some CDs have been used as a sensor for detecting and quantifying heavy metals (Tang et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2021) including Cr6+ (Bu et al., 2016). This shows that YM is useful in the preparation of iron oxide-based and CD materials, which are capable of adsorbing and detecting Cr6+. YM is a residue rich in silicon (Barbosa et al., 2020) and SiO2 is another material with interesting properties but its production from YM wastes has not been previously reported.

Although the use of biowastes for the synthesis of several nanomaterials has been extensively reported, the documents typically report the use of organic matter to obtain one core material without taking advantage of its versatility as feedstock to obtain several materials. Up to date, the authors are unaware of published studies that consider the obtaining of both materials, adsorbent, and sensor, from a single waste through serials synthesis processes nor its application on the Cr6+ adsorption process.

The economical aspect of the proposed environmental technology process, considering both the material synthesis and remediation process itself, is commonly disregarded in technical publications. Most reported studies do not consider the economic feasibility of their materials, they typically want to consider and study the adsorption yields without considering the economic aspect of the whole process.

This study aims to take advantage of the versatility of the YM wastes to produce several nanomaterials (or nanocomposites) to be explored for the first time in the Cr6+ adsorptive or detection/quantification and apply them to the remediation process of simulated Cr6+contaminated water, and a cost-benefit analysis using the unitary cost of the enhancement of adsorption,
φ
, of the use of YM in the synthesis and adsorption processes was explored.

The following section provides a comprehensive overview of the materials, reagents, equipment, and procedures utilized to achieve the study's goals. The accepted standard techniques are either briefly outlined or accompanied by relevant literature references.

FeSO4·7H2O (>99 wt %), FeCl3·6H2O (>97 wt %), NH4OH (30%), NaOH (>98 wt %), HNO3 (65 wt %), KOH (>98 wt %), K2Cr2O7 (<99.9 wt %) and 1,5-diphenylcarbazide (>99 wt %) denoted as DPC, were supplied by Sigma Aldrich and used without further purification. Type I water was obtained from a Synergy® water purification 137 system (18 MΩ). Commercial YM (dry leaf) was acquired from "El Playadito" production batch 45-03.

The experimental method used in this study produces several materials from a single YM batch to produce Fe3O4, CD, SiO2, and a composite using the former two. The synthesis route of nanomaterials is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Initially, an aqueous YM extract was prepared. Briefly, 20 g of YM were suspended in 1 L of distilled water and then heated at 50 °C under magnetic stirring at 350 rpm for 1 h. Then, the suspension was filtrated (
φ
= 0.22
μ
m) and posteriorly centrifuged at 1300 rpm for 15 min to remove any bulky particles. The resulting aqueous extract (infusion) and 17.9 g of solid residual YM were stored at 4 °C for 48 h before use.

The organic molecules in the infusion were used as surface coatings to protect previously synthesized magnetite nanoparticles (Magnacca et al., 2014). For that 6.1 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 4.2 g of FeSO4·7H2O were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water. The solution was heated to 90 °C, then 10 mL of NH4OH and 50 mL of YM extract were sequentially added. The resulting suspension was kept at 90 °C for 30 min stirring, then cooled down to 25 °C. The magnetic solid was separated using a laboratory magnet and washed three times with water. Finally, the powders were dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 12 h and then stored at 25 °C (the final mass obtained was 4.8 g). A YM-free material was obtained through the same procedure, except that 50 mL YM extract was replaced by pure water (final mass 4.4 g). The YM-coated and YM-free magnetite particles were named Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4.

Fe3O4 material was decorated with CD (vide infra). 0.3 g of Fe3O4 powder was suspended in 50 mL of the CD aqueous suspension and stirred for 30 min. The resulting suspension was placed in a stainless-steel hydrothermal autoclave reactor with a Teflon chamber and heated at 160 °C for 12 h. After that time, the autoclave was left to reach room temperature, and the solid was recovered using a laboratory magnet, washed three times with water, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The obtained material weight was 0.35 g and was labeled as Fe3O4@CD.

The residual YM-solid from the infusion production was used for the CD and SiO2 synthesis. For the CD, an adaptation of an own reported protocol was followed (Monje et al., 2021); 10 g were calcined with a 10 °C min−1 heating ramp until 420 °C and then maintained at that temperature for 2 h. The obtained coal was left to cool down at 25 °C (100 mg of coal was obtained) and later suspended in 1 L of distilled water. The resulting suspension was taken to ultrasonic vibration for 1 h at 25 ± 2 °C. The solid was separated from the supernatant by filtration with hydrophobic membranes (
φ
= 0.1
μ
m). The latter was subjected to centrifugation at 13000 rpm for 15 min and then stored at 3 °C. The obtained yellowish suspension contains the CD.

To obtain SiO2, all the residual coal from the previous steps was dried at 60 °C for 12 h and used as raw material to synthesize silica nanoparticles by adapting a method reported by Uruthira et al. (Kalapathy et al., 2000, 2002): 10 g of the coal was calcined at 700 °C for 12 h with a heating ramp of 10 °C min−1 and maintained there for 30 min. 0.5 g of ashes were obtained when the system was cooled down. To eliminate any trace metals on the ashes, they were suspended in 100 mL of NaOH 1N under vigorous agitation for 30 min. The latter produces the respective insoluble metal hydroxides. The suspension was filtrated (
φ
= 0.1
μ
m), and the solids were discarded. To promote the nucleation of the silica nanoparticles, the pH was adjusted to 5.0 with HCl and left at 25 °C and without agitation for 12 h. The resulting suspension was centrifuged to remove any bulky particles, and the gel-like supernatant was dried at 45 °C for 2 days. The obtained white color solids were sieved and washed three times with water and once with ethanol to remove any soluble impurities and finally dried at 100 °C for 4 h. This sample weight 0.5 g and was labeled as SiO2.

The particles morphology and size were studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a Tecnai F20 super twin TMP, with a field emission of 0.1 nm in 200 Kv, equipped with a GATAN US 1000XP-P camera. The GATAN Digital Micrograph software was used to analyze the obtained micrographs.

The crystalline phase content of the samples was assessed by X-ray Diffraction (XRD) using a PW3040/60 X'Pert PRO MPD X-ray diffractometer from PANalytical, in Bragg-Brentano geometry equipped with the high-power ceramic tube PW3373/10 LFF source with Cu anode. The qualitative phase analysis was performed with the EVA software package and compared to the ICDD- PDF-2- database. The crystal size was estimated by the Scherrer equation.

N2 adsorption−desorption isotherms at 77 K were measured with a 3FLEX Micromeritics apparatus as follows: 0.25 g of sample were outgassed overnight at 373 K for 12 h under a vacuum pressure of 15 Pa before running the analyses. The relative pressure (P/P0) range of the measurements was between 0.01 and 0.99. The BET-specific surface area (SBET) of the materials was calculated following the IUPAC recommendations.

The functional groups in the prepared materials were identified by their IR spectrum obtained with a Bruker IFS28 spectrophotometer equipped with a Globar source and a DTGS detector working at 128 scans with a 4 cm−1 resolution in the overall range of 4000 to 400 cm−1. Samples were prepared by pressing a grounded mixture of 1% of the particle powder with 99% dry KBr at a load of 5 tons.

Raman spectra were collected using a Renishaw Ramascope instrument equipped with a 10 mW 532 nm laser (SpectraPhysics) working at 10% power with a 30 s acquisition time, a Rayleigh filter, with 1200-line mm−1 grating, and a SYNAPSE CCD model 354010 (Horiba) detector.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained under UHV with an XR50 Specs GmbH spectrometer using Mg K(α) as the excitation source and a PHOIBOS 100 half-sphere electron energy analyzer. Two-point calibration of the energy scale was performed using sputtered cleaned gold (Au 4f7/2, binding energy (BE) 84.00 eV) and copper (Cu 2p3/2, BE: 932.67 eV) samples. Internal calibration to correct for surface charging was performed with the C 1s peak at BE = 284.6 eV due to adventitious carbon. High-resolution XPS spectra were taken to get a better insight into the chemical environment of the different atoms. A Shirley-type background from each spectrum was used to remove the effect of the extrinsic structure loss. The spectrum was resolved by Gaussian- Lorentzian fitting, keeping χ2 values to their minimum values.

A suspension of each material was obtained in water, and the ionic strength was adjusted to 0.05 M by using KNO3. A series of suspensions with pH in the range of 2–12, by adding adequate aliquots of KOH or HNO3 1 M solutions to portions of the corresponding mother suspension. All the suspensions were agitated on a rotatory shaker at 25 °C and 350 rpm for 24 h. The ζ-potential was measured on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS for each suspension.

Thermal gravimetric analyses (TGA) were done using a Q600 from TA Instruments. The program used an air environment and involved a 1-min initial temperature of 50 °C, followed by a 10 °C min−1 ramp up to 800 °C.

Acid−base titrations were done with a Schott Titroline Alpha titrator equipped with a 20 mL burette syringe and connected to a Schott Blueline 11-pH glass combination electrode. The system was controlled using Pytrator software. The end points of the titration curves were determined from the maximum on the first derivative curve. Reported results for each sample are the statistical average of at least three replicates.

Due to their intrinsic luminescence properties, the CDs were characterized by steady-state and time-resolved emission and anisotropy experiments. The formers were carried out in a HORIBA-YVON Spex Fluorolog FL3-11, applying the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique using a quartz cell with a 10 mm path length. The excitation source was a 450 W Xenon lamp and monochromated with a 1 nm bandpass gap for selecting the excitation and emission wavelength. All experiments were performed at 25.0 ± 0.1 °C, controlled with an F-3004 Peltier sample cooler controlled by an LFI-3751 temperature controller (Wavelength electronics). For the time-resolve emission anisotropy, a pulsed LED (λEM = 341 nm – FWHM 400 ps) and the emission were analyzed at 448 nm using a detector TBX-100. The time-resolved spectra of the LED were obtained from a Ludox suspension (SM-30, 30% SiO2 in water pH = 10.2 with a mean diameter of 7 nm and 345 m2 g−1, Sigma–Aldrich).

Two different processes were used to quantify Cr6+ concentration. First, Uv/Vis spectroscopy was used to follow the metal-organic complex formed by soluble Cr6+ with DPC. The experimental protocol is described in detail in our previous report (Rangel et al., 2021). The second protocol was based on the luminescence properties of the obtained CD (vide infra). For that purpose, 3 mL of the CD aqueous suspension were mixed with 1 mL of a Cr6+ solution and magnetically stirred for 5 min, and the pH was established at 6.0 with a Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM). The determination of the experimental conditions is described in the analysis and results section.

The adsorption experiments were done in batch systems. Variable volumes of a K2Cr2O7 stock solution of 1000 mg L−1 were mixed with 5 mL of 100 ppm solution of either Fe3O4, Fe3O4@YM, Fe3O4@CD, or SiO2, and then the suspensions were further diluted to a final volume of 25 mL. The pH was adjusted to 6.2–6.5 by using HNO3. The vials were agitated in a rotatory shaker at 400 rpm, at 25 ± 2 °C, for 2 h to reach the adsorption/desorption equilibrium (vide infra). The solids were separated through centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 15 min, and then the final Cr6+ concentration was measured.

The weight of adsorbed pollutant in mg per g of adsorbed qe was calculated using the equation (Eq.) (1):

The intensive enhancement price for the adsorption process is a useful parameter to analyze the economic feasibility of the whole process on the Cr6+maximum adsorption uptake, φ (mg Cr6+ USD−1) shown in Eq. (7) (Rangel et al., 2021).

Δqm represents the difference in the qm from the Langmuir adsorption model (vide infra) between the respective material and the bare-YM (59.6 mg Cr6+ g−1) (Mazzeo et al., 2020). The production cost (USD g−1) is the cost associated with synthesizing and purifying the corresponding material considering the synthesis yields, reagents, and energy inputs, among other parameters. For easier visualization, the production cost is shown in USD Kg−1, but when applying Eq. (7), the units should be in (USD g−1) as previously mentioned.
Finally, the reuse of materials with the best cost performance was evaluated using distilled water at pH 5.0 for pollutant desorption. This procedure was repeated for five cycles.
This section presents a thorough analysis of the results obtained from the study investigation. The findings are extensively discussed.

The HRTEM micrographs, shown in Fig. 2 and Figure SM1 (supplementary material), corroborate that all are in the nanoscale. The use of a carbon-based grid did not allow the acquisition of suitable micrographs for pure CD, due to the relatively low contrast. Our group reported the synthesis of CD from YM through a similar route, with typical spherical geometry with a mean size of 5.1 ± 1.5 nm (Monje et al., 2021). These results confirm that all the obtained particles are nanomaterials with sphere-like geometry.

Although the agglomeration of the particles in the micrographs was evident, it was possible to estimate the mean diameter. Fe3O4, Fe3O4@YM, Fe3O4@CD, and SiO2 showed values of 15.2 ± 1.3, 14.8 ± 1.6, 17.6 ± 2.1, and 19.7 ± 1.9 nm (Table 1 ). When comparing the values for Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@YM, is evident that using YM as an additive in the synthesis does not modify the mean diameter, but a difference is elucidated on Fe3O4@CD, which is associated with CD surface adsorption on top of the iron oxide (vide infra). Regarding the iron-based materials, the HRTEM micrographs show plane spacing of ca. 2.5 Å in agreement with the d311 plane of magnetite/maghemite phases.

For Fe3O4, a non-crystalline surface region with a mean width of 1.4 ± 0.3 nm is elucidated which might be attributed to surface defects in the crystalline domains since the crystalline planes observed in bulk are not present in the surface layer. For Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD, a wider amorphous surface layer with a mean width of 2.4 ± 0.3 and 4.1 ± 0.5 nm is observable. These surface layers are attributable to adsorbed organic molecules and CD (Li et al., 2019; Magnacca et al., 2014). The surface domain has spherical-like geometry in agreement with the morphology for pure CD.

Taking advantage of the CD luminescent properties (vide infra), a non-microscopic approach was used to estimate their size. The corresponding emission anisotropy decay curve is shown in Figure SM 2. Mono-exponential functions adjusted the decay satisfactorily, suggesting that the fluorescence emission originated from a particle population with three identical perpendicular rotational axes, which confirms the spherical geometry. The experimental values for θ and r0 are 1.32 ns and 0.37, corresponding to a mean diameter of 4.5 nm (Section 1.1 - Eqns SM1 and SM2). The obtained diameter is in concordance with the HRTEM micrographs observed here and with the typical diameter observed for CD.

The XRD diffractograms for Fe3O4, Fe3O4@YM, Fe3O4@CD, and SiO2 are shown in Figure SM 3. The iron-containing materials, show six diffraction peaks at 30.3° (2 2 0), 35.4° (3 1 1), 43.2° (4 0 0), 53.7° (4 2 2), 57.2° (5 1 1), and 63.2° (4 0 0) which could be attributable to either maghemite or magnetite phase. The Scherrer equation was used to estimate the average crystalline domain sizes considering the peak at 35.4°, with 14.9, 11.5, and 12.5 nm for Fe3O4, Fe3O4@YM, and Fe3O4@CD. The decreases in the crystalline domains using the organic coatings suggest that the passivation processes decrease the iron oxide crystalline domain size. The coincidence between these values and the obtained through HRTEM micrographs (Fig. 2) suggests that all iron-based materials are single-crystal particles surrounded by a disordered layer of iron oxide which in turn is coated with the YM-derived molecules or CD. Regarding the SiO2, its respective diffraction pattern evidences the amorphous nature of this sample (Kalapathy et al., 2002).

Table 1 also shows the SBET, evidencing that upon functionalization with either YM or CD, the specific surface area increases from 38.1 to 72.3 and 202.2 m2 g−1 for Fe3O4, Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD. The marked increase in the last sample might be due to the formation of the organic shell corresponding to the agglomeration of CD on the surface of the iron oxide. Regarding the SiO2, the specific surface area was 27.5 m2 g−1.

The FTIR spectra are shown in Fig. 3 (a). For the sample Fe3O4, the band at 568 cm−1 is characteristic of Fe–O stretching, the same signal is observed at 595 and 618 cm−1 for Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD which suggests the formation of surface organometallic complexes between iron and the organic molecules from the YM extract or the CD. The signals at 1562 and 3406 cm−1 are attributed to δH–O–H and νOH vibrations of lattice and physisorbed water molecules on the Fe3O4 sample. These bands are also observed for Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD, due to the nature of the coatings, the contribution of N–H groups should not be neglected.

For Fe3O4@YM the bands in 1726 and 1078 cm−1 are attributable to CO and C–OH vibrations, suggesting –COOH groups on the surface. The band at 1250 cm−1 is assigned to the C–O–C vibrations. The peak at 1631 cm−1 suggests the presence of physisorbed water molecules, but the contribution of CC groups should not be neglected and the peaks at 2926 and 2842 cm−1 suggest the presence of C–H groups (from –CH2 or –CH3), confirming the presence of a surface organic layer for this sample.

When compared to the Fe3O4@YM, Fe3O4@CD shows similar peaks attributed to the same functional groups, with some differences: the small peak at 1391 cm−1, which suggests the presence of surface -C-OH corresponding to either carboxyl groups or tertiary alcohols in graphene-like structures. When qualitatively comparing the spectra of both samples, the latter has a major contribution of -C-H groups, around 2929 and 2854 cm−1, which could be attributed to the graphitic-like structure reported for the CD (vide infra).

SiO2 shows the typical vibration mode belonging to Si–O bonds. The strong bands at 1089 and 488 cm−1 are due to Si–O–Si vibration modes, and the undefined shoulder around 960 cm−1 belongs to the Si–OH vibration modes, confirmed by the presence of the band at 3430 cm−1 assigned to the O–H groups (Feifel and Lisdat, 2011). The absence of the vibrational signals attributed to organic functional groups suggests the complete carbonization of the organic matter in the hydrochar used as a raw matter to obtain the SiO2 particles.

Fig. 3 (b) shows the Raman spectra. Fe3O4 spectrum shows two bands at 330 and 668 cm−1 attributed to the magnetite phases, corresponding to the T2g and A1g phonon modes. For Fe3O4@YM, the latter phonon modes are also observable, suggesting the preservation of the magnetite phase after the adsorption of organic molecules from the YM extract. But new signals are observable when compared to Fe3O4. The bands at 454 and 705 cm−1 might suggest hematite and maghemite domains, which might suggest the oxidation of the pure magnetite domains in the Fe3O4 upon incorporating the organic compounds from the YM extract, but YM extract has antioxidant properties (Jubb and Allen, 2010), which refute the previous hypothesis. The evidenced oxidation might take place due to the interaction of the sample with the lase during the analysis(Shebanova and Lazor, 2003). A relatively intense broad band centered around 1451 cm−1 is also observable for Fe3O4@YM, likely due to organometallic complexes. Raman signals around this region have been reported for the CC bond in the structure -Fe-O-CC- resulting in the adsorption of pyrocatechol-like structures on magnetite nanomaterials. But also due to carboxylic acid moieties on the same iron phase due to the structure –COO–Fe-(Hurst et al., 2011; Lee et al., 2015). In agreement with the TEM and IR analyses, this last signal strongly suggests the presence of organic molecules at the surface.

For Fe3O4@CD, three signals are observable at 344, 518, and 710 cm−1, suggesting maghemite and magnetite domains co-existence. Two new bands centered at 1371 and 1570 cm−1, denoted as ID and IG, are observable, which indicate the presence of CD. The ID band corresponds to sp3 hybridized carbon edge belonging to defects in the graphitic structure, and IG to the CD graphitic layers structure (Brachi, 2020). This result suggests a difference in the surface composition between Fe3O4@CD and Fe3O4@YM. The spectrum for SiO2 shows the characteristic bands: D1, D2, SiO2 network, and surface SiOH at 495, 604, 798, and 978 cm−1 (Alessi et al., 2013).

The X-ray photoelectron spectra of the samples were recorded, and some are shown in Figure SM 4. The iron-containing samples all depict the main lines for Fe, O, C, and N. The nitrogen content in the Fe3O4 might be derived from the NH4OH used in the synthesis. The Fe2p peak was simulated by three individual signals and two satellites, showing an invariant maxima position in all samples, with ΔBE = 13.4 eV, which is in concordance with the Fe–O bonds. The first peak is located at 710.4 eV, attributed to Fe2+, and the other two peaks, around 712.1 and 715.1 eV, are attributable to Fe3+ (Biesinger et al., 2011). This agrees with the crystal structure of magnetite, which is mixed-valence iron oxide represented by Fe2+O·Fe3+ 2O3 (Canevari et al., 2017).

The maxima binding energies obtained here differ from macroscale magnetite particles, but they agree with the values reported for nanoscale magnetite (Wilson and Langell, 2014). The theoretical Fe2+/Fe3+ ratio for magnetite is 0.5, and the experimental ratio for Fe3O4 is 0.52, but for Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD, the ratios are 1.21 and 1.23. This might be due to the antioxidant properties of the YM aqueous extract.

Considering the experimental sensitivity factors relative to the different elements identified by the XPS spectra, Fe3O4, Fe3O4@YM, and Fe3O4@CD have surface composition: Fe1O1.5N0.2, Fe1O1.4N1.2, and Fe1O1.7N1.4. These results indicated that the surface passivation with either YM or CD modifies the surface composition substantially.

Only Si and O were detected on SiO2, which confirms the absence of organic matter on the material. The Si2p and O1s spectrums and their speciation curves are shown in Figure SM 5 and Figure SM 6. The Si2p signal has two contributions located at 103.2 and 104.7 eV, attributed to Si–O–Si and Si–OH. The latter surface groups correspond to the outer atomic layer of the SiOx coatings defects (Post et al., 2018). The O1s spectrum (not shown) has two contributions; the first one, located at 532.7 eV, is in concordance with the Si2p signals, corresponding to the Si–O–Si environment. The second peak identified at 532.1 eV denotes surface CO which could be due to the carbonate-like impurities on the surface (Post et al., 2018). Considering the Si and O species on SiO2 structures, the experimental ratio Si/O is 0.43, which aligns with the 0.5 theoretical value. The binding energies according to XPS of some materials are listed in Table SM 1.

The ζ-potential profiles over the pH of all materials in aqueous suspension are shown in Fig. 4 (a). Fe3O4 and SiO2 profiles are in concordance with the literature (Kosmulski, 2016), with isoelectric points (IEP) of 6.8 and 3.1. The surface charge depends on the pH due to the acid/base superficial group dissociation. Eq. (8) and (9) represent the mentioned phenomena, where ≡Me-represents a surface iron or silicon atom and whether –OH, -O- or –OH2 + corresponds to specific the terminal group (Cloarec et al., 2016):
Eq. (8) ≡Me–OH + H3O+ ⇌ ≡Me–OH2 + + H2O
Eq. (9) ≡Me–OH + HO− ⇌ ≡Me–O- + H2O

For pH < IEP, the protonation of Me–OH occurs, producing surface ≡Me–OH2 + groups, as shown in Eq. (8). For Above pH > IEP, the number of ≡Me–O- sites increases due to the Me–OH deprotonation, represented in Eq. (9). Conversely, at the isoelectric point, the number of ≡Me–OH2 + and ≡Me–O- sites are equal, resulting in a net-zero charged surface. Regarding Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD, they both have a negatively charged surface under all the considered pH values, in concordance with the literature (Magnacca et al., 2014).

Table 1 also shows that the organic matter (OM) from YM provides carboxylic and phenolic groups, which were measured by acid-base titration. R–COOH (vide supra) typically has pKa in the range of 3.0–5.0, resulting in surface R-COO- groups at pH = 6.5. Phenolic-like groups (R–OH) with pKa > 7.0 might also go under deprotonation-producing R-O- surface groups at the same pH. However, Fe3O4@CD showed a higher concentration of both groups concerning Fe3O4@YM, which might be due to the additional oxidant hydrothermal treatment to synthesize the nanocomposite. At pH = 6.5 (vertical green dashed line), the materials show ζ-potentials with values of 9.3, −25.3, −35.9, and −17.7 mV for Fe3O4, Fe3O4@YM, Fe3O4@CD, and SiO2 (Fig. 4 (a) and Table 1).

The thermograms in Fig. 4 (b) from TGA analyses show a typical behavior for Fe3O4 and SiO2, with a total mass loss of 1.9 and 4.1%. The weight loss from 100 to 190 °C is due to the desorption of physisorbed water molecules. For temperatures above 190 °C, the dehydroxylation of surface metal hydroxides takes place. When compared to the bare Fe3O4, an increase in weight loss of 10.2 and 19.1% is observed for Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD. These changes occur in the region between 200 and 500 °C, which is attributed to surface organic matter oxidation. Thermal decomposition of hemicellulose, cellulose, and lignin from YM occurs in the same region (Chen and Kuo, 2010). The latter results suggest that for Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD the shell of the original Fe3O4 surface with organic matter is produced by either YM aqueous extract or CD.

Fig. 5 (a) shows that the yellowish pure CD suspension has luminescence upon irradiation with UV light (λEXC = 350 nm). Fig. 5 (a) also shows the fluorescence spectra of the obtained CD, with a strong blue component emission. The excitation-emission matrix of the CD is shown in Figure SM 7 (a), and it might be seen that the maximum emission intensity is found at λEM = 448 nm when irradiated at λEXC = 370 nm.

The effect of ions on the CD fluorescence intensity was evaluated using the optimized fluorescence conditions (λEXC = 370 nm and λEM = 448 nm) and as evidenced in Figure SM 7 (b) and (c), and specifically for this material, anions do not quench the fluorescence, but some cations do. Ni2+, Co2+, and Pb2+ showed a relatively high fluorescence quenching effect on the synthesized CD. The most predominant quenching effect is observed for Cr6+, and as shown in Fig. 5 (b), an experimental correlation between the quenching and this metal concentration suggests that CD are suitable sensors for detecting Cr6+ in waters at pH = 6.0. The analytical regression obtained for the calibration curve is (I0/I) = 0.00565 [Cr+6] + 0.98110 for the range between 1.00 and 243.00 μM, with a detection limit of 0.58 μM. Table 2 summarizes the linear range and limit of detection of several fluorescence-based materials with reported Cr6+ detection capability. The CD obtained in this study is coherent with the properties previously reported in the literature. The previous analytic data obtained from the CD fluorescence-based method was verified by ICP-OES and UV/Vis spectroscopy (Section 1.2).

The detection mechanism is under discussion at the current state of the art. Some studies (Huang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018) suggest that the Cr6+ species acts as an inner filter (Inner filter effect) to reduce the absorbed photon intensity and decreasing the respective emission intensity. Huang et al. (2019) suggested that the adsorption of Cr6+ onto the CD surfaces produces a non-radiative complex.

Based on these results, CD showed to be interesting material for detecting Cr6+ in water. To the best of the authors knowledge, a single agro-industrial feedstock, such as YM, has not been reported to produce sensors and adsorbent materials on a single synthesis route. In the next section, Fe3O4, Fe3O4@YM, Fe3O4@CD, and SiO2 will be used as Cr6+ adsorbents.

The kinetic aspect of the adsorption process was assessed (Fig SM 8). Fe3O4, Fe3O4@YM, Fe3O4@CD, and SiO2 were evaluated on the adsorption of different Cr6+ concentrations in the range of 0.32–51.20 mg L−1 using a fixed dose of the materials (0.1 g L−1). The experimental equilibrium time is approximately 45 min. The kinetic profiles were evaluated using pseudo-first and pseudo-second order kinetic models; the results are reported in Table SM 2. A better fit (R2 > 0.99) for the pseudo-second order kinetics was observed in all cases. This suggests that the Cr6+ adsorption mechanism occurred in two elemental steps.

The experimental Cr6+ adsorption isotherms are shown in Fig. 6 , the lines correspond to the fitting of the Langmuir, Freundlich, or Redlich- Peterson isotherm model, and Table 3 summarizes the corresponding fitting parameters. The Langmuir adsorption model fits better with the experimental data (R2 > 0.99) for all cases, which supports the formation of an adsorption monolayer.

Regarding the parameters of the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, the affinity coefficient, KL, shows the trend SiO2 ≈ Fe3O4 ≫ Fe3O4@YM ≈ Fe3O4@CD, which suggests that the nature of the interaction between the Cr6+ ions and the surfaces of SiO2 and Fe3O4 is stronger when compared to the Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD. The latter results might suggest different adsorption mechanisms for the two groups, which in nature elucidates different ion-surface interactions. When dissolved in water at pH = 6.5, Cr6+ is reported as HCrO4 −, Cr2O7 2−, and CrO4 2−, and at this pH, Fe3O4 has a net positive charge (vide supra). Based on these results, the system containing Fe3O4 as an adsorbent an ionic interaction between the metallic anionic species and ≡Fe–OH2 + groups are plausible. The latter analysis should also be applied for SiO2, the ζ-potential analysis only considers a mean surface charge, but even at pH < IEP, ≡Si–OH2 + might still be present on the surface.

For Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD, a lower KL value is observed, suggesting that the Cr6+-surface interaction is weaker when compared with the SiO2 or Fe3O4. The proposed mechanism for the Cr6+ adsorption on negatively charged organic surfaces follows an initial reduction of Cr6+ to Cr3+ and subsequent complexation of the cationic trivalent species with oxygen-containing surface groups (Li et al., 2017).

For the number of adsorption sites, qm, the experimental trend is Fe3O4@CD > Fe3O4@YM ≫ Fe3O4 > SiO2 (Table 3). The latter results are coherent with the just mentioned mechanism. The relatively low qm for SiO2 is associated with the predominance of negative charges on the surface and a negligible contribution of -Si-OH2 + groups on the surfaces since the IEP for this particle is < 6.2. The difference observed for Fe3O4@CD and Fe3O4@YM might be attributed to the higher –COOH and Ph-OH concentrations and SBET belonging to the first material (Table 1) Although the proposed mechanism is the same for these two materials, the functional group concentration and the available surface area for this process heavily impact the adsorption capacity of each material.

The experimental qm of SiO2, Fe3O4, Fe3O4@YM, and Fe3O4@CD for Cr6+ removal were compared with other materials recently reported in the literature, and the results are shown in Table 4 . Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD presented a qm superior to most evaluated materials, including graphene oxides, metal-doped biochars, activated carbon, and Fiber ball. Although the reported Magnetic Zr-MOF has a higher qm (428.60 mg g−1) when compared to Fe3O4@CD (312.50 mg g−1), the considered pH in the first study is 3.0, which is not neat circumneutral pH conditions and the neutralization step might negatively affect the economic feasibility of the process. From these results, it is possible to indicate that Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD, are efficient alternatives for the adsorption of Cr6+. The last analysis does not consider that CD nanoparticle, which can act as a sensor of Cr6+, are also obtained during the implementation of the considered synthetic route in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cost estimate, production performance, and reusability evaluation of developed materials during Cr6+ removal
ce:section.ce:para: A relatively good technical performance of a material as an adsorbent does not guarantee that its implementation on a pilot or industrial scale is suitable. Aspects such as the estimated production cost of the materials and their technical performance should be also considered. Rangel et al. (2021) introduced the parameter φ as the intensive enhancement price on the maximum adsorption uptake, which considered both previously technical and economic scenarios. The mentioned parameter evaluates the impact of a synthesis or modification procedure to increase the number of adsorption sites, qm, concerning the raw material, as it is YM in this case, divided by the total production cost of the respective material.

Table 5 reports the respective qm regarding Cr6+ adsorption and the production cost of all studied materials. The latter parameter was obtained using all the values shown in Table SM3 . Fe3O4 has the lowest production cost (8.89 USD kg−1). Using YM as feedstock or additive under the experimental conditions here considered, results in an increase of the production cost up to 12.10 USD kg−1 for Fe3O4@YM and 43.76 USD kg−1 for Fe3O4@CD. SiO2 is the material with the highest production cost (541.91 USD kg−1) due to the high energy requirement for the calcination process. Table 5 also reports the decreasing trend Fe3O4@YM > Fe3O4@CD > SiO2 > Fe3O4 for the parameter φ, although regarding the number of Cr6+ adsorption sites, qm, the trend is Fe3O4@CD > Fe3O4@YM > Fe3O4 > SiO2. The difference in these two trends suggests that the material with higher technical performance regarding the adsorption process does not necessarily correspond with the one with the highest economic feasibility associated with its synthesis process. These results highlight the importance of including this type of cost analysis in research.

Fe3O4 and SiO2 exhibit
φ
< 0, which inditates that regarding the Cr6+ adsorption, the use of raw YM material is economically more favorable than using the mentioned materials. This suggests that obtaining Fe3O4 and SiO2 for the Cr6+ adsorption process lacks economic support. Fe3O4@CD exhibits the highest qm 312.50 mg g−1, and its respective
φ
is 5.8 mg Cr6+ USD−1. Here it is easy to understand that investing in the synthesis and purification process of Fe3O4@CD results in an increase of the Cr6+ adsorption capacity of 5.8 mg Cr6+ per USD invested during the synthesis conditions reported here. Interestingly, although Fe3O4@YM only showed qm 161.29 mg Cr6+ g−1, it showed the highest
φ
, with a value of 8.4 mg Cr6+ USD−1, which is 44.5% higher than the observed for Fe3O4@CD. This suggests that from the economic aspect, Fe3O4@YM is the most favorable adsorbent considering the synthesis and application process.

Attempts to compare these materials technical-economic performance, based on φ, with those shown in Table 5 were unsuccessful. It was impossible to calculate φ due to the lack of information on these studies. It is necessary to improve the information reported in adsorption studies that allow scientific progress in the economic field, thereby, the industrial sector will have details on a product's cost-benefit relationships, allowing it to make the right decisions regarding its implementation on a real scale.

Reusing adsorbent materials is an important characteristic that should also be evaluated for real applications. The reuse of the materials having the higher qm and
φ
(Fe3O4@CD and Fe3O4@YM) was studied, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 . Although Fe3O4@CD and Fe3O4@YM decrease the maximum adsorption capacity by around 15% between the first and second cycles, qm remains almost constant up to the fifth cycle in both cases.

The latter results agree with the previously discussed Cr6+ adsorption mechanism. Although after an adsorption cycle, the surface is saturated with Cr6+, after the regeneration step, which consists of the several sonication steps of the dried adsorbent on water pH = 5.0. The desorption on the metal takes place due to the exchange between H+ and the adsorbed Cr3+, releasing the adsorption sites, which are available for further Cr6+ adsorption (Alhumaimess et al., 2019). Based on the experimental results shown here, Fe3O4@CD and Fe3O4@YM showed interesting alternatives to remove Cr6+ during several cycles.

Several nanomaterials were successfully prepared through a single biowaste using serial processes. All materials were spherical. Fe3O4 and SiO2 do not have an organic layer, but Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD do. Specifically for the latter material, the deposition of CD on the surface was elucidated. Pure CD showed a sensor capacity to quantify solubilized Cr6+; the analytical data presented in this study was obtained using this material for simulated water bodies. The application of this sensor on real water matrixes is limited due to the interference of Cr6+ detection with Ni2+, Co2+, and Pb2+.

Regarding the Cr6+ adsorption process, kinetically, the process follows a pseudo-second order, suggesting that the adsorption occurs in two steps, which based on the literature, for the Fe3O4@YM and Fe3O4@CD, corresponds to the Cr6+ reduction to Cr3+ and posterior Cr3+ surface complexation. The Langmuir isotherm adsorption model fit suggests that the materials have a homogeneous surface regarding the Cr6+ adsorption process, which occurs through forming a monolayer, Fe3O4@CD, the one with the highest number of adsorption sites. When considering the economic feasibility of the whole process, Fe3O4@YM showed a higher performance.
This study demonstrates that YM is a suitable bioplatform for environmental and nanotech interests.
